proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that 5% of the city's population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily participate in a single-day event without significant logistical friction. The technical requirement for a custom app to track routes and calculate GHG savings in real-time, while citing existing algorithms, presents a non-trivial development and data privacy hurdle not addressed in the plan. Furthermore, the causal chain assumes that a one-day behavioral change will automatically trigger complex, long-term systemic shifts (e.g., 2% of bus lines converting to electric, 1% city demand covered by solar) without intermediate policy mechanisms or funding guarantees.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is well-established globally, with the proposal explicitly citing similar programs in the US and Canada. The novelty is limited to the specific aggregation of these activities with a proposed 'Big Data' tracking app and the specific context of Buenos Aires. The proposed algorithm for GHG calculation is described as a derivative of existing government footprint calculators, offering no new scientific or technical methodology.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) is mathematically plausible based on the stated participation rate, though the 5% participation assumption is a significant uncertainty. However, the long-term impact projections are scientifically unsound due to a lack of causal evidence. The proposal attributes massive systemic changes (e.g., 35,700 tonnes of annual savings from electric buses, 70,205 tonnes from solar) directly to a single awareness event. These outcomes depend on independent legislative and capital investment processes that cannot be causally linked to a one-day walk event with the confidence implied. The extrapolation to global impacts (39 million tCO2e/year) compounds these errors by assuming linear scalability of behavioral change and policy adoption across diverse geopolitical contexts.

presentation.rationale: The proposal contains significant technical inconsistencies and formatting issues that undermine its scientific credibility. There are contradictory statements regarding transportation modes (e.g., 'skip the bus' in the summary vs. 'incentivize public transport' later). The long-term impact section conflates correlation with causation, attributing the results of broad infrastructure laws (solar, electric buses) to the specific event. Additionally, the text includes broken references (e.g., '[14]', '[15]'), image placeholders, and grammatical errors that suggest a lack of rigorous review.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal functions more as a marketing campaign than a scientifically grounded intervention. While the short-term behavioral goal is plausible, the causal mechanisms linking a one-day event to long-term infrastructure and legislative changes are tenuous and unsupported by evidence. The quantitative impact model suffers from severe attribution errors, inflating the proposal's potential by counting systemic outcomes that would likely occur independently or require distinct policy drivers.
key_concerns: Causal Attribution Error: The proposal incorrectly attributes long-term infrastructure outcomes (electric bus adoption, solar law coverage) to a single-day awareness event without defining the intermediate policy or funding mechanisms.

Participation Uncertainty: The 5% participation rate (approx. 145k people) is a critical assumption with no empirical basis provided; actual turnout for voluntary single-day events is typically much lower.

Data Privacy and Technical Feasibility: The plan requires a custom app to track individual routes and calculate emissions, raising significant data privacy concerns and technical development costs not accounted for.
Contradictory Messaging: The proposal simultaneously encourages skipping public transport ('skip the bus') and incentivizing its use, creating a logical inconsistency in the behavioral model.

Scalability Assumptions: The global extrapolation assumes that behavioral changes and policy outcomes in Buenos Aires will linearly scale to 1 billion people across diverse economic and regulatory environments.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on unrealistic institutional assumptions, specifically that the government will 'arrange with transportation about the modification of their routes' for a single-day event and that major tech firms (Google) will develop a custom tracking app without a formal procurement process. The governance model assumes voluntary compliance from employers for 'home office' mandates without legal or regulatory levers to enforce this, and the reliance on a one-off event to trigger complex legislative outcomes (e.g., passing a solar law) lacks a credible implementation pathway.

novelty.rationale: The concept of a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day is a well-established global practice (referenced in the proposal itself regarding US/Canada). While the integration of a specific app for real-time GHG tracking and the attempt to link it to 'Big Data' for urban planning adds a slight layer of modernization, the core governance mechanism remains a standard awareness campaign rather than a novel policy instrument.

impact.rationale: The projected long-term impacts are institutionally disconnected from the proposed action. The proposal claims a single awareness day will lead to the approval of a 'Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law' covering 5% of demand or a 2% implementation of electric buses. These are complex, multi-year legislative and infrastructure projects that cannot be causally attributed to a one-day walking event. The short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e) are negligible at a city scale, and the extrapolation to global impacts (39 million tCO2e) is mathematically flawed and ignores the lack of enforcement mechanisms for habit change.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard sections (Business Model Canvas, Impact/Benefits) and attempts to use data. However, the presentation of the governance logic is weak; it conflates 'awareness' with 'policy implementation' and presents speculative long-term legislative outcomes as direct results of the event. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') and the informal tone regarding government mandates ('The government will notificate...') detract from its professional credibility as a policy proposal.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory perspective, this proposal functions more as a public relations campaign than a viable policy intervention. It lacks the institutional depth to bridge the gap between a one-day event and the complex regulatory changes (solar laws, bus electrification) it claims to drive. The feasibility is low due to the assumption of ad-hoc government cooperation and the absence of a sustainable funding or enforcement model for the proposed app and behavioral changes.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes the government can unilaterally modify public transport routes for a single day without a formal regulatory or operational framework.
There is no mechanism to enforce or incentivize the 'home office' mandate for private companies; this relies entirely on voluntary goodwill.
The causal link between a one-day walking event and the passage of complex legislation (Solar Law) or infrastructure rollout (Electric Buses) is logically unsound.
The proposal relies on a custom app developed by a major tech giant (Google) without a defined procurement, data privacy, or data ownership strategy.
The long-term impact calculations appear to conflate the event's direct output with unrelated, long-term national policy trends.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big bang' single-day event model with unrealistic dependencies: government authorization to modify bus routes, Google developing a custom tracking app, and mass corporate adoption of home office for a one-off day. The operational complexity of coordinating security, health support, and media for a city-wide walk without a clear pilot phase or budget breakdown makes immediate execution highly risky.

novelty.rationale: The core concept (walking/biking to work days) is a well-established global practice (e.g., Bike to Work Day, Earth Hour). The proposal adds little operational novelty, merely suggesting the aggregation of existing behaviors into a single day with a proposed app that mirrors existing footprint calculators.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are mathematically derived, they are negligible in the context of global or even city-wide emissions. The long-term impact projections (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally) are based on highly speculative behavioral shifts (10% recycling increase, 2% electric bus adoption) that are not causally linked to a single walking event, rendering the impact model optimistic and ungrounded in operational reality.

presentation.rationale: The proposal outlines a clear vision and identifies key stakeholders (government, NGOs, sponsors). However, the presentation of the business model and cost structure is fragmented, relying on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') and lacking a concrete financial plan or risk mitigation strategy for the identified operational hurdles.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' that lacks the operational scaffolding for scalability. It assumes high-level government and corporate cooperation that is difficult to secure for a one-day event without a proven pilot. The reliance on a custom app and the extrapolation of long-term systemic changes from a single day of walking represent significant execution risks. The model needs to shift from a massive one-off event to a scalable, recurring program with lower coordination overhead.
key_concerns: Dependency on government to modify public transport routes and authorize a city-wide event is a high-risk bottleneck.
Assumption that Google will develop a custom tracking app for a single event is operationally unrealistic.
Lack of a detailed budget for security, health support, and media, which are critical for a city-wide event.
The causal link between a one-day walking event and long-term systemic changes (e.g., electric bus adoption, solar law approval) is weak and speculative.
Scalability is hindered by the need for high-level coordination; a decentralized, app-led approach without government route changes would be more feasible.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'one-day' event to drive long-term behavioral change and assumes 5% of the population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily walk or bike to work in a city with significant infrastructure gaps. The requirement for government authorization to modify bus routes and for companies to mandate home office days for a single event is administratively heavy and unlikely to be approved on short notice. Furthermore, the assumption that a smartphone app can accurately track 'avoided' trips (e.g., someone who would have driven but now walks) without baseline data is technically fragile.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (referenced in the proposal itself regarding US/Canada). While the integration of a specific app for data collection is a standard feature of modern civic tech, the core mechanism does not offer a novel approach to climate action or urban mobility compared to existing 'Earth Hour' or 'Car-Free Day' initiatives.

impact.rationale: The projected impact is highly optimistic and potentially misleading. The calculation of 39 million tCO2e/year for the world relies on a linear extrapolation of a single day's event to a year, which is mathematically unsound for behavioral change. On the ground, a single day of reduced traffic may not result in measurable long-term habit changes without sustained infrastructure support. The proposal also overlooks that for many low-income residents in Buenos Aires, walking 10-15 blocks is not a choice but a necessity, and skipping public transport (as suggested) could isolate those who rely on it for essential services.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the tone is overly idealistic and lacks critical engagement with the realities of the target population. The text contains several grammatical errors and typos (e.g., 'algoritm', 'obteined', 'kilometrers') that undermine its professional credibility. The logic connecting a one-day event to a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of solar laws is tenuous and not well-argued.

A4.perspective_summary: From the perspective of the affected community, this proposal feels like a top-down campaign that romanticizes walking while ignoring the daily struggles of those who cannot easily swap their commute. The 'skip the bus' suggestion is particularly problematic for low-income workers who rely on affordable public transit. While the intent to raise awareness is positive, the plan lacks the grounded understanding of urban mobility barriers, safety concerns for pedestrians/cyclists, and the socioeconomic reality that 'walking to work' is often a sign of poverty, not a voluntary eco-choice.
key_concerns: The suggestion to 'skip the bus' and 'subway' is dangerous for low-income populations who rely on these for affordable access to jobs and healthcare; walking long distances is not a viable alternative for everyone.
Safety concerns are not addressed: Buenos Aires has specific safety issues for pedestrians and cyclists, especially at night or in certain neighborhoods, which the proposal ignores.
The '5% participation' target assumes a level of privilege (living near work, having a safe route, having a smartphone) that excludes a large portion of the city's population.
The data collection model assumes participants will accurately self-report 'avoided' trips, which is prone to gaming and inaccuracy without verified baseline data.
The proposal treats 'walking' as a universal solution without acknowledging that for many, the lack of safe sidewalks and crosswalks makes walking impossible or dangerous.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that the government will mandate or strongly enforce 'home office' and modify public transport routes for a single-day event. The logistical burden of coordinating security, health support, and mass media for a voluntary event without a clear operational plan for the 'government authorization' bottleneck makes execution risky. Furthermore, the reliance on a specific app (potentially Google) to track data and calculate emissions introduces a dependency that may not materialize.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. While the proposal attempts to integrate data tracking and corporate sponsorship, the fundamental mechanism is a standard awareness campaign. The novelty is further diminished by the lack of a unique value proposition beyond existing 'Earth Hour' or 'Bike to Work' initiatives referenced in the text.

impact.rationale: The proposal presents a severe normative conflict between its stated goals and its calculated impact. The short-term savings (66 tCO2e) are negligible on a global scale, and the long-term projections (39 million tCO2e) rely on a 'slippery slope' assumption that a one-day event will permanently alter infrastructure laws (solar PV), recycling rates, and bus electrification. The proposal hides the risk that the 'awareness' generated may not translate into structural change, while the exclusion of those who cannot walk or bike (due to disability, distance, or safety) is not adequately addressed in the impact assessment.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation of the 'value generation loops' and business model is vague. The calculations for long-term impact are presented with false precision (e.g., specific tonnage from hypothetical policy changes) without explaining the causal link between a one-day walk and the approval of a 'Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law.' The text is readable but lacks the rigorous normative justification required to support its ambitious claims.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to leverage a one-day behavioral nudge to trigger systemic policy changes, but it obscures the significant distributional risks and the lack of a credible causal mechanism for its long-term impact claims. While the intent to promote health and sustainability is positive, the plan risks excluding vulnerable populations who cannot walk or bike, and it places an unrealistic burden on government and corporate actors to alter infrastructure and work policies based on a single event. The 'awareness' goal is noble, but the proposal fails to address who bears the cost of participation and who is left behind.
key_concerns: Exclusion risk: The proposal assumes all participants can walk or bike, ignoring those with disabilities, chronic illnesses, or those living in areas with unsafe pedestrian infrastructure.

Normative trade-off: The proposal conflates 'awareness' with 'structural change,' assuming a one-day event will automatically lead to the approval of complex laws (solar PV) and infrastructure upgrades (electric buses) without a detailed transition plan.

Data privacy and equity: The reliance on a smartphone app to track movement and calculate emissions may exclude low-income populations without smartphones or data plans, creating a 'digital divide' in who can participate and win prizes.
False causality: The long-term impact calculations assume a direct causal link between the event and major policy shifts (e.g., 5% solar coverage) that are not supported by the proposal's logic.

Dependency on voluntary compliance: The success of the 'home office' component relies entirely on employer cooperation, which is not guaranteed and may disproportionately affect low-wage workers who cannot work remotely.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a fragile chain of dependencies for long-term impact: it assumes a single-day event will trigger a 10% increase in recycling, a 5% adoption of distributed solar, and a 2% shift to electric buses over five years. These systemic infrastructure and behavioral shifts require years of policy work, not a one-off awareness day. Furthermore, the reliance on government authorization to modify bus routes and enforce home-office days for the entire city is a significant political and logistical hurdle that makes the projected scale of impact highly improbable without a much more robust, multi-year implementation plan.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). While the proposal attempts to integrate a smartphone app for data tracking, the fundamental mechanism—using a single day of voluntary behavior change to drive long-term systemic transformation—is not novel. The approach treats complex, deep-seated urban mobility and energy systems as problems solvable by a temporary awareness campaign.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact (66 tCO2e/day) is negligible in the context of global or even city-wide emissions. The long-term impact projections are critically flawed due to a 'single-event fallacy'; the proposal assumes that a one-day event will causally lead to massive, sustained changes in recycling rates, solar adoption, and electric bus infrastructure. This ignores the inertia of urban systems and the need for continuous reinforcement. The proposal risks creating a 'feel-good' illusion of impact without building the actual resilience or structural changes required for intergenerational sustainability.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data to support its claims. However, the presentation of the long-term impact calculations is misleading, presenting speculative correlations (e.g., 'awareness day' -> 'solar law approval') as direct causal outcomes. The reliance on external entities (Google, Government) to execute critical parts of the plan without a detailed operational roadmap weakens the overall coherence of the proposal from a systems perspective.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term sustainability lens, this proposal represents a classic 'awareness trap': it focuses on a high-visibility, low-leverage event while assuming it will magically resolve deep systemic issues like infrastructure deficits and entrenched consumption habits. While the immediate reduction in emissions is positive, the projected long-term benefits are speculative and lack a mechanism for resilience. The proposal fails to address the second-order effects of urban planning or the time scales required for genuine cultural and infrastructural shifts, risking a shift of the problem rather than a solution.
key_concerns: The causal link between a one-day event and long-term systemic changes (solar adoption, electric bus implementation, recycling rates) is unsupported and likely overestimated.
The proposal assumes government and corporate cooperation (home office mandates, route changes) without detailing the political or economic incentives required to secure them.
There is no strategy for maintaining behavioral change after the event day ends, which is critical for long-term resilience.
The calculation of long-term GHG savings conflates the event's direct impact with unrelated policy outcomes that would likely occur (or not) regardless of the event.
The reliance on a single day of data collection to inform city-wide infrastructure planning (e.g., subway entrances) may yield skewed or non-representative data.

clarification_requests.question: You identified a 'Causal Attribution Error' regarding the link between the event and long-term infrastructure changes. Could you specify which intermediate policy mechanisms or funding guarantees would be minimally required to make this causal chain plausible, so we can assess if the proposal can be amended to include them?

You highlighted that the 'skip the bus' suggestion is dangerous for low-income populations. Does the proposal explicitly state that participants must skip public transport, or is this a misinterpretation of the text? If it is explicit, what specific alternative mobility solutions for low-income residents do you recommend to make the event inclusive?

You noted the lack of a mechanism to enforce 'home office' mandates. If the proposal were revised to replace the mandate with a voluntary incentive structure (e.g., tax breaks or corporate recognition), would you consider the feasibility score higher, and what specific incentive model would you suggest?

You described the proposal as an 'awareness trap' due to the lack of a strategy for maintaining behavioral change. What specific 'second-order' interventions or continuous reinforcement mechanisms would you propose to bridge the gap between a one-day event and the long-term systemic changes (solar, electric buses) the proposal aims to trigger?

clarification_responses.question: You identified a 'Causal Attribution Error' regarding the link between the event and long-term infrastructure changes. Could you specify which intermediate policy mechanisms or funding guarantees would be minimally required to make this causal chain plausible, so we can assess if the proposal can be amended to include them?
clarification_responses.answer:
To render the causal chain between a single-day awareness event and long-term infrastructure changes (e.g., 2% electric bus fleet, 1% solar coverage) scientifically plausible, the proposal must explicitly integrate specific intermediate mechanisms that bridge the gap between 'awareness' and 'capital investment/policy enactment.' Currently, the proposal treats these outcomes as automatic consequences of the event, which is a logical fallacy in systems dynamics.

The minimal required mechanisms to amend the proposal would include:

1.  **Formalized Policy Advocacy Pathway:** Instead of assuming the government will act, the proposal must detail a pre-defined advocacy strategy. This includes a formal commitment from the event organizers to present a specific policy brief to the City Legislature or Ministry of Transport within 30 days of the event, utilizing the 'Big Data' collected as evidence for specific route optimizations or infrastructure needs. The causal link requires: Event Data -> Policy Brief -> Legislative Hearing -> Budget Allocation.

2.  **Public-Private Partnership (PPP) Framework:** For the electric bus and solar targets, the proposal must identify a funding mechanism. A one-day event cannot generate the capital required for fleet electrification. The proposal needs to specify a mechanism such as a 'Green Bond' issuance triggered by the event's visibility, or a pre-negotiated Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with utility companies and bus operators where the event serves as the 'social license' to unlock existing budget lines or attract specific climate finance (e.g., Green Climate Fund).

3.  **Behavioral Persistence Metrics:** To justify long-term impact claims, the proposal must shift from a 'one-day' metric to a 'retention' metric. It requires a mechanism to track participants over 6-12 months to demonstrate a statistically significant shift in modal share (e.g., a 5% permanent reduction in car usage among participants). Without longitudinal data, the assumption that a single day creates a 'habit' is empirically weak.

4.  **Regulatory Sandbox or Pilot Authorization:** For the 'Big Data' to influence infrastructure (e.g., new subway entrances), the proposal must include a mechanism for the government to designate the event data as a 'regulatory pilot' that legally obligates the transport authority to review specific route proposals based on the collected data.

Without these explicit intermediate steps, the causal claim remains a 'black box' assumption. The proposal currently lacks the engineering of the policy process, relying instead on the hope that awareness alone drives capital-intensive infrastructure change. If the proposal were amended to include a specific advocacy timeline, a defined funding vehicle (e.g., a matched grant), and a longitudinal tracking protocol, the feasibility and impact scores could be revised upward, as the causal mechanism would move from 'magical thinking' to 'plausible policy intervention.'

clarification_responses.question: You highlighted that the 'skip the bus' suggestion is dangerous for low-income populations. Does the proposal explicitly state that participants must skip public transport, or is this a misinterpretation of the text? If it is explicit, what specific alternative mobility solutions for low-income residents do you recommend to make the event inclusive?
clarification_responses.answer:
You are correct to press for clarification on this point. Upon re-reading the text, the proposal is indeed explicit and contradictory in its instructions. In the 'Summary' section, it states: 'skip the bus, avoid the subway and turn to their own bodies... by walking.' However, in section (a) under 'What actions do you propose?', it immediately contradicts itself by saying: 'Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way.' 

This confusion is not just a typo; it reflects a fundamental disconnect from the reality of the ground. For a low-income resident in Buenos Aires (e.g., living in a villa or a distant suburb like La Matanza), 'skipping the bus' to walk 10-15 blocks is not a 'choice' or a 'healthy habit'—it is often physically impossible due to distance, or dangerous due to lack of safe sidewalks. If they interpret the 'skip' instruction literally, they are being asked to abandon their only affordable link to the city center. If they follow the 'use public transport' instruction, they are violating the 'skip' rule.

To make this inclusive, the proposal must explicitly reframe the goal: 'Do not skip public transport; instead, combine it with active mobility.' The specific alternative I recommend is to focus the event on the 'last mile' problem. We should encourage people to take the bus/subway to the nearest safe station and then walk the final 10-15 blocks, rather than asking them to walk the entire commute. Furthermore, for those who cannot walk at all due to disability or distance, the event should celebrate 'reducing car trips' specifically, rather than penalizing public transit use. The app should track 'car-free trips' (whether by bus, bike, or foot) rather than 'walking distance' exclusively. This shifts the narrative from 'walking is the only green way' to 'leaving the car at home is the green way,' which is the only interpretation that includes the working poor.

clarification_responses.question: You noted the lack of a mechanism to enforce 'home office' mandates. If the proposal were revised to replace the mandate with a voluntary incentive structure (e.g., tax breaks or corporate recognition), would you consider the feasibility score higher, and what specific incentive model would you suggest?
clarification_responses.answer:
Yes, replacing the 'home office' mandate with a voluntary incentive structure would significantly improve the feasibility score, moving it from a 2 to a 3. The original proposal's reliance on a government 'notification' to enforce home office days ignores the legal and political constraints of private sector labor relations in Argentina. A shift to incentives aligns with established policy frames such as 'soft governance' or 'nudging,' which are far more realistic for a pilot event.

Specific incentive models I would suggest include:
1. **Fiscal Incentives:** Offering a temporary, modest corporate tax credit or social security contribution reduction for companies that formally certify a 'Walk & Work' day where a specific percentage of staff telecommute. This requires legislative backing but is a standard mechanism in many jurisdictions.
2. **Regulatory Fast-Tracking:** Offering priority processing for building permits or environmental compliance renewals for companies that participate, leveraging the city's administrative capacity rather than direct mandates.
3. **Public Recognition & Branding:** Creating a formal 'Sustainable Commuter Partner' certification awarded by the City Government, which companies can use in their ESG reporting and marketing. This leverages the 'reputation' lever mentioned in the proposal's business model.

However, even with this revision, the feasibility score remains capped at 3 (not 4 or 5) because the proposal still lacks a defined funding mechanism for the app development (relying on Google's goodwill is not a governance strategy) and the causal link between a single event and long-term legislative outcomes (like the Solar Law) remains institutionally weak. The 'home office' fix addresses one major governance gap, but the broader implementation plan still lacks the necessary regulatory scaffolding for the app and the long-term impact claims.

clarification_responses.question: You described the proposal as an 'awareness trap' due to the lack of a strategy for maintaining behavioral change. What specific 'second-order' interventions or continuous reinforcement mechanisms would you propose to bridge the gap between a one-day event and the long-term systemic changes (solar, electric buses) the proposal aims to trigger?
clarification_responses.answer:
To bridge the gap between a one-day 'awareness' event and the deep systemic shifts required for intergenerational resilience, the proposal must evolve from a 'campaign' into a 'catalyst for structural feedback loops.' A single day cannot build infrastructure, but it can generate the political capital and data density necessary to justify long-term investment. Here are the specific second-order interventions I would propose to make this viable:

1.  **Data-Driven Infrastructure Advocacy (The 'Proof of Concept' Loop):** Instead of just tracking steps for a 'prize,' the app must be designed to identify 'mobility deserts' and 'safe-walk corridors' in real-time. The long-term mechanism is not the walking itself, but the *data product* sold to the city. The proposal should explicitly commit to using the event's data to lobby for permanent bike lanes or sidewalk expansions in the specific high-traffic zones identified. This shifts the value from 'emissions saved on Day 1' to 'permanent infrastructure unlocked by Day 2.'

2.  **Institutionalizing the 'Home Office' Norm:** The proposal mentions home office as a one-off. To build resilience, the intervention must target *policy change* within the participating corporations. The 'prize' for companies should not just be marketing, but a formal commitment to a 'Sustainable Mobility Policy' (e.g., a permanent 1-day/week remote work mandate or a subsidy for e-bike purchases). This creates a recurring reduction in emissions rather than a one-time spike.

3.  **The 'Green Dividend' Feedback Loop:** To address the solar and electric bus goals, the proposal needs a mechanism where the 'savings' calculated on the day are not just numbers, but a funding trigger. For example, a portion of the corporate sponsorship or a specific municipal tax credit could be tied to the *volume* of participants, creating a direct financial incentive for the city to accelerate the electric bus rollout or solar subsidies. This links the behavioral event to the capital investment cycle.

4.  **Community Stewardship Networks:** Awareness fades; community does. The event should launch 'Walking Committees' in each neighborhood that continue to meet monthly to audit local safety and advocate for local improvements. This builds the social capital and resilience needed to sustain the behavior change beyond the event.

Without these mechanisms, the event remains a 'feel-good' anomaly. With them, it becomes a legitimate pilot for a systemic transition, transforming the 'awareness trap' into a 'policy accelerator.'

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a fragile chain of dependencies, including government authorization to modify bus routes for a single day, voluntary corporate mandates for home office, and the development of a custom tracking app by a major tech firm without a procurement plan. While clarification exchanges suggested that shifting to voluntary incentives (e.g., tax breaks) could improve feasibility to a 3, the core operational model remains high-risk due to the lack of a defined budget, the absence of a pilot phase, and the unrealistic assumption that a one-day event can trigger complex legislative and infrastructure changes without intermediate policy mechanisms.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, explicitly referenced by the proposal itself. The integration of a smartphone app for data tracking is a standard feature of modern civic tech but does not constitute a novel methodology for climate action. The proposal attempts to aggregate existing behaviors rather than introducing a new mechanism for systemic change, and the proposed algorithm for GHG calculation is a derivative of existing government tools.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact (approx. 66 tCO2e/day) is mathematically plausible but negligible at a city scale. The long-term impact projections are critically flawed due to a 'single-event fallacy,' attributing massive systemic changes (e.g., 35,700 tonnes from electric buses, 70,205 tonnes from solar) directly to a one-day awareness event. The proposal fails to provide a causal mechanism linking the event to these outcomes, and the global extrapolation (39 million tCO2e/year) relies on unsound linear scaling assumptions. Clarification exchanges highlighted that without specific advocacy pathways and funding mechanisms, these impact claims remain speculative.

presentation.rationale: The proposal contains significant technical inconsistencies, including contradictory instructions to 'skip the bus' versus 'use public transport,' which undermines its logical coherence. The text includes broken references, image placeholders, and grammatical errors. Furthermore, the presentation of long-term impact calculations conflates correlation with causation, presenting speculative policy outcomes as direct results of the event without rigorous justification. While the structure attempts to use data, the lack of professional polish and logical gaps reduce its credibility.

final.synthesis: The expert consensus is that while the intent to raise climate awareness is positive, the proposal functions more as a marketing campaign than a scientifically grounded intervention. All experts (A1-A6) agreed on low scores for Novelty (2) and Impact (2) due to the lack of innovation and the 'single-event fallacy' in impact modeling. Feasibility and Presentation were also rated low (2) due to operational risks, contradictory messaging, and the absence of a realistic implementation plan. The clarification exchanges reinforced these views: A1 emphasized the need for formal policy pathways to bridge the gap between awareness and infrastructure; A4 highlighted the exclusionary nature of the 'skip the bus' instruction for low-income populations; A2 noted that shifting to voluntary incentives could slightly improve feasibility but not enough to overcome the lack of a funding model; and A6 stressed the need for 'second-order' interventions to ensure behavioral persistence. The final judgment integrates these perspectives, concluding that the proposal requires a fundamental redesign to move from a one-off event to a sustainable, inclusive, and causally linked policy pilot.
final.uncertainty: The primary uncertainty lies in the potential for the proposal to be amended. If the organizers were to replace the 'skip the bus' instruction with a 'last mile' focus, implement a formal advocacy pathway with defined policy mechanisms, and secure a funding model for the app and incentives, the feasibility and impact scores could potentially rise to 3 or 4. However, as currently written, the causal links remain unproven, and the operational risks are high. There is also uncertainty regarding the actual participation rate, as the 5% target is optimistic and unverified.
